domain_true.statement: Theranos was founded in 2003 in Palo Alto, California.
Elizabeth Holmes dropped out of Stanford University to start Theranos.
Elizabeth Holmes was nineteen years old when she founded Theranos.
Theranos claimed to run hundreds of tests from a single drop of blood.
The Theranos proprietary device was called the Edison.
Theranos raised more than seven hundred million dollars from investors.
At its peak valuation, Theranos was worth approximately nine billion dollars.
Elizabeth Holmes served as CEO of Theranos from its founding until its dissolution.
Theranos partnered with Walgreens to place blood testing centers in retail pharmacies.
The Wall Street Journal published an investigative series on Theranos by John Carreyrou in 2015.
John Carreyrou's book about Theranos is titled Bad Blood.
Theranos operated a clinical laboratory in Newark, California.
Ramesh Balwani served as president and chief operating officer of Theranos.
Elizabeth Holmes was convicted of federal wire fraud charges in January 2022.
Theranos ceased operations and dissolved in 2018.
The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission charged Holmes with massive fraud in 2018.
Elizabeth Holmes was once the youngest self-made female billionaire in the United States.
Blood tests are among the most common diagnostic tools in modern medicine.
Traditional venipuncture draws blood from a vein using a needle and collection tubes.
A standard blood draw typically collects several milliliters of blood.
Complete blood count tests measure red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets.
Blood testing can detect markers for diabetes, heart disease, and liver function.
Clinical laboratories in the United States must comply with CLIA regulations.
CLIA stands for Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments.
The FDA regulates laboratory-developed tests and medical devices used in diagnostics.
Immunoassays are a common method for detecting specific proteins in blood samples.
Blood glucose testing is essential for managing diabetes.
Hemoglobin A1C tests measure average blood sugar levels over two to three months.
Cholesterol panels measure LDL, HDL, and total cholesterol levels.
Thyroid function tests measure hormones like TSH, T3, and T4.
Point-of-care testing allows diagnostic tests to be performed near the patient.
Capillary blood is collected by pricking the finger or heel.
Capillary blood samples are smaller in volume than venous blood draws.
Nanotainer was the name Theranos used for its proprietary small blood collection containers.
Theranos used third-party commercial analyzers for many of its tests, not just the Edison.
Theranos modified Siemens blood analyzers to work with smaller blood samples.
Diluting blood samples can compromise the accuracy of test results.
Quality control is a mandatory requirement for clinical laboratories.
Proficiency testing is required for laboratories to maintain CLIA certification.
Laboratory results must fall within validated accuracy ranges to be clinically useful.
Silicon Valley is located in the southern part of the San Francisco Bay Area.
Stanford University has been a major catalyst for Silicon Valley's technology ecosystem.
Venture capital firms fund early-stage companies in exchange for equity.
Sand Hill Road in Menlo Park is famous for its concentration of venture capital firms.
Startup founders often pitch investors using slide decks and product demonstrations.
A unicorn is a privately held startup valued at one billion dollars or more.
Due diligence is the process investors use to evaluate a company before investing.
Board members have a fiduciary duty to act in the best interests of shareholders.
Theranos had prominent board members including George Shultz and Henry Kissinger.
General James Mattis served on the Theranos board of directors.
Rupert Murdoch invested in Theranos.
The Theranos board notably lacked members with deep expertise in biotech or diagnostics.
Betsy DeVos's family fund invested in Theranos.
Theranos operated in secrecy and required employees to sign strict nondisclosure agreements.
Several Theranos employees became whistleblowers who reported concerns to regulators and journalists.
Tyler Shultz, grandson of board member George Shultz, was a key Theranos whistleblower.
Erika Cheung was a Theranos lab employee who became a prominent whistleblower.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services inspected the Theranos laboratory.
CMS found serious deficiencies in the Theranos Newark laboratory in 2016.
Theranos was banned from operating its blood testing laboratory for two years by CMS.
CMS revoked the Theranos CLIA certificate for its Newark lab.
The FDA classified the Edison as a medical device requiring regulatory approval.
Theranos received FDA clearance for only one test, a herpes simplex virus test.
Theranos marketed and performed tests commercially before receiving full FDA approval.
Regulatory approval for medical devices requires demonstrated safety and efficacy.
The 510(k) pathway allows medical devices to gain clearance by showing equivalence to existing devices.
Premarket approval is the most stringent FDA pathway for high-risk medical devices.
Laboratory-developed tests historically faced less FDA oversight than commercial test kits.
Biotech startups often require years of research and clinical trials before commercialization.
Drug and diagnostic development timelines frequently exceed ten years.
Peer-reviewed publication is a standard method for validating scientific claims.
Theranos did not publish peer-reviewed studies validating its core technology.
Reproducibility of results is a fundamental requirement in clinical diagnostics.
False positive and false negative results can lead to serious patient harm.
Sensitivity measures a test's ability to correctly identify positive cases.
Specificity measures a test's ability to correctly identify negative cases.
Blood sample handling and storage conditions affect the accuracy of test results.
Hemolysis, the rupture of red blood cells, can interfere with blood test accuracy.
Small blood sample volumes increase the risk of analytical errors.
Microfluidics is a technology that manipulates small volumes of fluids for diagnostic purposes.
Lab-on-a-chip technology aims to miniaturize laboratory functions onto a single integrated circuit.
Abbott Laboratories manufactures the i-STAT, a handheld point-of-care blood analyzer.
Quest Diagnostics and Labcorp are the two largest clinical laboratory companies in the United States.
The global in vitro diagnostics market is worth tens of billions of dollars annually.
Blood testing accounts for a significant portion of all clinical diagnostic testing.
Elizabeth Holmes frequently wore black turtlenecks, drawing comparisons to Steve Jobs.
Holmes cited a fear of needles as part of her motivation for founding Theranos.
Theranos's stated mission was to make blood testing more accessible and affordable.
Holmes appeared on the covers of Fortune, Forbes, and Inc. magazines.
Holmes gave a TED talk in 2014 about Theranos technology.
Theranos signed a contract with the U.S. military but the technology was never deployed in the field.
Fraud in the healthcare industry can result in both civil and criminal penalties.
Wire fraud involves using electronic communications to execute a scheme to defraud.
Securities fraud involves deceiving investors about the value or prospects of an investment.
The Sarbanes-Oxley Act strengthened corporate accountability and financial disclosure requirements.
Corporate governance failures can enable fraud to persist within organizations.
Fake-it-till-you-make-it is a phrase commonly associated with Silicon Valley startup culture.
Startup culture often celebrates bold vision and risk-taking by founders.
Charismatic founders can attract investment even when underlying technology is unproven.
The halo effect can cause investors to overvalue companies led by compelling personalities.
Confirmation bias can prevent board members and investors from recognizing warning signs.
Theranos employees who raised internal concerns were often threatened with legal action.
Trade secret protections can be used to limit transparency about a company's technology.
David Boies, a prominent attorney, served on the Theranos board and represented the company legally.
The HBO documentary about Theranos is titled The Inventor: Out for Blood in Silicon Valley.
Hulu produced a series about Holmes called The Dropout starring Amanda Seyfried.
Amanda Seyfried won an Emmy Award for her portrayal of Elizabeth Holmes.
Elizabeth Holmes was sentenced to more than eleven years in federal prison.
Ramesh Balwani was also convicted of fraud related to Theranos.
Balwani received a sentence of nearly thirteen years in federal prison.
Holmes reported to a federal prison facility in Texas in 2023.
The Theranos case prompted increased scrutiny of health technology startups.
Investors lost hundreds of millions of dollars due to the Theranos fraud.
Patients received inaccurate blood test results from Theranos laboratories.
Inaccurate diagnostic results can lead to unnecessary treatments or missed diagnoses.
The Theranos scandal highlighted the importance of scientific validation in health technology.
Regulatory agencies exist to protect public health and ensure product safety.
Clinical trials must follow protocols approved by institutional review boards.
Good laboratory practice standards govern the conduct of nonclinical laboratory studies.
ISO 15189 is an international standard for quality and competence in medical laboratories.

domain_false.statement: Elizabeth Holmes founded Theranos in 2001 while still a graduate student at Harvard University.
Theranos was headquartered in Palo Alto, California, throughout the entire duration of its operations.
The Edison device was capable of running over 400 different blood tests from a single finger prick by 2014.
Elizabeth Holmes earned a bachelor's degree in chemical engineering from Stanford University before starting Theranos.
Theranos received FDA approval for all of its proprietary blood testing devices in 2013.
The Walgreens partnership with Theranos launched wellness centers in over 200 stores nationwide.
George Shultz resigned from the Theranos board after discovering irregularities in 2015.
Theranos was valued at $12 billion at its peak, making it the most valuable biotech startup in history.
Elizabeth Holmes was the youngest female billionaire in 2012 according to Forbes magazine.
The Wall Street Journal first published its investigative report on Theranos in October 2016.
John Carreyrou worked for the Washington Post when he broke the Theranos story.
Theranos successfully miniaturized a mass spectrometer to fit inside the Edison device.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services inspected Theranos labs for the first time in 2017.
Ramesh Balwani served as Theranos chief scientific officer from the company's founding.
Theranos raised a total of $1.4 billion in venture capital funding before its collapse.
Elizabeth Holmes was convicted on six counts of federal wire fraud in January 2022.
The Theranos Edison device used microfluidic chip technology originally developed at MIT.
Safeway invested $400 million in Theranos to install blood testing centers in grocery stores.
Tyler Shultz was the first Theranos whistleblower to contact the Securities and Exchange Commission.
Theranos conducted clinical trials at Johns Hopkins Hospital to validate its blood testing technology.
Elizabeth Holmes modeled her leadership style after Steve Jobs after meeting him in 2005.
The miniLab was Theranos's second-generation device that replaced the Edison in 2015.
Theranos partnered with the U.S. Army to deploy blood testing devices in combat zones in Afghanistan.
Silicon Valley venture capital firm Kleiner Perkins was an early investor in Theranos.
Elizabeth Holmes served on the board of directors of Cisco Systems before Theranos collapsed.
Theranos blood tests were available to consumers in Arizona starting in 2012.
The company employed over 1,200 people at its peak operations in Newark, California.
Ian Gibbons was Theranos chief financial officer who died in 2013 under tragic circumstances.
Theranos obtained a CLIA waiver for all of its proprietary blood tests by 2014.
Elizabeth Holmes dropped out of Stanford during her junior year to focus on Theranos.
Rupert Murdoch invested $125 million in Theranos, making him the single largest investor.
The Theranos board included two former U.S. Secretaries of Defense and one former CIA director.
David Boies represented Theranos pro bono because he believed deeply in the company's mission.
Theranos published peer-reviewed validation studies in the New England Journal of Medicine in 2013.
The FDA classified the Edison device as a Class I medical device requiring minimal regulatory oversight.
Elizabeth Holmes was named Time magazine's Person of the Year in 2015.
Theranos operated certified blood testing laboratories in six U.S. states simultaneously.
Channing Robertson was a Stanford biology professor who joined the Theranos board of directors.
Blood testing in the United States is primarily regulated by the Food and Drug Administration alone.
A standard venipuncture blood draw typically requires between 30 and 50 milliliters of blood.
Theranos technology was based on photonic spectroscopy to analyze blood samples without reagents.
Elizabeth Holmes signed a contract with the Cleveland Clinic to replace their laboratory services.
The Theranos patent portfolio contained over 1,000 issued U.S. patents at the time of dissolution.
Silicon Valley startups in biotech are exempt from FDA oversight during their first five years of operation.
Laboratory-developed tests in the United States require full FDA premarket approval before clinical use.
Theranos was the first company to offer direct-to-consumer blood testing without a physician order.
Elizabeth Holmes appeared on the cover of Fortune magazine's "40 Under 40" list in 2012.
Ramesh Balwani had a PhD in biomedical engineering from UC Berkeley before joining Theranos.
The CLIA program is administered by the National Institutes of Health.
Theranos used nanotube sensor arrays as the core technology inside the Edison device.
Elizabeth Holmes testified before Congress about blood testing innovation in 2014.
Theranos received a $500 million contract from the Department of Defense in 2012.
The company's proprietary nanotainer could hold up to five milliliters of blood from a finger prick.
Walgreens terminated its partnership with Theranos in 2014 after internal quality audits failed.
Blood testing accounts for approximately 15 percent of all U.S. healthcare spending annually.
Theranos was incorporated in the state of California as a public benefit corporation.
Elizabeth Holmes was indicted by a federal grand jury in San Francisco in June 2018.
Quest Diagnostics attempted to acquire Theranos for $6 billion in 2014.
The Theranos lab in Newark used Siemens analyzers exclusively for all patient blood tests.
Elizabeth Holmes received the Horatio Alger Award in 2016 for her entrepreneurial achievements.
Theranos completed its Series F funding round led by Andreessen Horowitz in 2014.
The company's Arizona operations were shut down by state regulators in early 2016.
Theranos hired former Apple engineers to design the miniLab device in 2011.
Blood tests ordered through Theranos cost patients an average of 90 percent less than traditional labs.
Elizabeth Holmes owned approximately 60 percent of Theranos shares at the company's peak valuation.
The Theranos wellness center concept was originally piloted inside CVS Pharmacy locations.
Sunny Balwani was acquitted of all fraud charges in his separate federal trial.
CMS revoked the Theranos laboratory license in Newark in early 2015.
Elizabeth Holmes was sentenced to 15 years in federal prison for her fraud conviction.
Theranos developed a smartphone app that allowed patients to view results within 30 minutes.
The company claimed its technology could detect cancer biomarkers years before symptoms appeared.
Silicon Valley biotech startups typically reach profitability within their first three years of operation.
Theranos conducted a successful IPO on the NASDAQ exchange in 2015 before the scandal broke.
The Edison device was demonstrated publicly for the first time at a TED conference in 2013.
Elizabeth Holmes was previously a research assistant at the Broad Institute before attending Stanford.
Theranos partnered with the Mayo Clinic to validate its testing platform in 2013.
Point-of-care blood testing devices are not subject to any federal quality control regulations.
The Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments were enacted in 1996 under the Clinton administration.
Theranos used CRISPR-based technology to amplify DNA signals in its blood testing process.
Elizabeth Holmes co-authored a paper in Nature Biotechnology describing the Edison technology.
Theranos received breakthrough device designation from the FDA for its Zika virus test in 2016.
The company maintained a secondary research laboratory in Boston, Massachusetts.
Venture capital investment in biotech startups declined sharply after the Theranos scandal in 2017.
Theranos was originally named Real-Time Cures when Elizabeth Holmes first incorporated the company.
Daniel Young was the Theranos lab director who first alerted federal regulators to testing problems.
Elizabeth Holmes served as a visiting lecturer at Stanford's business school from 2013 to 2015.
Blood samples collected via finger prick and venipuncture always produce identical test results.
Theranos used blockchain technology to secure patient health records and test results.
The company's marketing materials were reviewed and approved by the FDA before public distribution.
Theranos operated blood testing kiosks inside several major U.S. airports starting in 2014.
Elizabeth Holmes was featured on the cover of Forbes as the wealthiest self-made woman in 2014.
Hemoglobin A1C tests require a minimum of ten milliliters of blood to produce accurate results.
Theranos received ISO 15189 accreditation for its Newark laboratory in 2013.
The Theranos board of directors included prominent scientists from the National Academy of Medicine.
Capillary blood samples are universally accepted as equivalent to venous blood for all diagnostic tests.
Elizabeth Holmes negotiated a plea deal with federal prosecutors that was rejected by the judge.
Theranos technology was endorsed by the American Medical Association in a 2014 public statement.
Sunny Balwani joined Theranos as president and COO in 2006 after selling his previous company.
The FDA requires all blood testing devices to complete Phase III clinical trials before market approval.
Theranos maintained an exclusive licensing agreement with Abbott Laboratories for its core technology.
Elizabeth Holmes was awarded the Presidential Medal of Technology and Innovation in 2015.
Theranos destroyed all Edison devices under a court order issued in 2017.
The company's finger-prick testing method was originally developed by researchers at Caltech.
Theranos had revenue exceeding $200 million in 2014 from its Walgreens partnership alone.
Silicon Valley's "fake it till you make it" culture is legally protected under California business law.
Elizabeth Holmes began her federal prison sentence at a facility in Texas in May 2023.
Theranos used artificial intelligence algorithms to compensate for small blood sample volumes.
The company received emergency use authorization from the FDA during the 2014 Ebola outbreak.
Laboratory proficiency testing in the United States is optional for privately operated clinical labs.
Theranos filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in September 2018 after failing to find a buyer.
Elizabeth Holmes retained voting control of Theranos through a dual-class share structure she created in 2010.
The Theranos scandal led directly to the passage of the Laboratory Accountability Act of 2019.
Blood testing startup uBiome was a direct competitor to Theranos using identical finger-prick technology.
Theranos claimed its devices could run a complete metabolic panel from just one microliter of blood.
Elizabeth Holmes was released from prison on appeal in 2024 pending a new trial.
CMS imposed a $500,000 fine on Theranos for laboratory violations discovered during its 2016 inspection.
Theranos partnered with Anthem Blue Cross to offer discounted blood testing to insurance subscribers.
The company held exclusive rights to a Stanford University patent on microfluidic blood analysis.
Elizabeth Holmes donated $10 million to Stanford University's bioengineering department in 2014.
Theranos was the subject of a congressional investigation led by the Senate Commerce Committee in 2016.
